text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 20 May 2023 

Dr Aisha Rahman  
St George’s University Hospital  
Department of Neurology, Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT  

Dear Dr Aisha Rahman,  

Michael Carter, DOB: 23-02-1992, Hospital No: H673021 NHS No. 9475201836  
Flat 12, 8 Brookside Lane, London SE15 3QJ 

Diagnoses: 

Combined generalised and focal epilepsy (patient has both generalised and focal epilepsy)  
EEG (March 2023): intermittent generalised spike–wave discharges with additional left temporal sharp waves  
MRI brain (April 2023): no focal structural lesion identified 

Current Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily  
Lacosamide 100 mg twice daily 

Plan: 

- Continue current antiepileptic regimen without dose change  
- Reinforce adherence strategies using smartphone alarms and NFC tag check-ins at dosing sites  
- Provide rescue plan: buccal midazolam 10 mg for generalised convulsive seizures lasting >5 minutes; family trained and supplied  
- Bloods today (U&Es, LFTs) to monitor therapy tolerance; results to be shared with GP  
- Driving: patient not driving and understands DVLA regulations given ongoing seizures  
- Review in 6 months, sooner if breakthrough clusters or adverse effects 

I reviewed Michael Carter in clinic today accompanied by a family member. They report mixed seizure types: brief focal impaired-awareness episodes with behavioural arrest and oral automatisms, and occasional generalised tonic–clonic seizures occurring predominantly out of sleep. The family feel seizure frequency has decreased markedly since starting medication, with only seven seizures so far this year, down from monthly generalised events last year. No injuries have been reported, and there have been no emergency department attendances in the past three months.

They described a history of missed evening doses earlier in the year; since then, they have implemented smartphone alarm schedules and placed NFC tags next to the pill organiser and bedside table. These prompt dose confirmation twice daily and have been helpful in maintaining adherence. No significant adverse effects on the current regimen—mild transient dizziness with Lacosamide initiation has resolved. Sleep hygiene has improved and the patient is limiting alcohol and avoiding sleep deprivation.

On examination today, neurological assessment is unremarkable. Recent EEG demonstrated both generalised and focal features, and MRI brain was normal. Given the clinical improvement, we will continue Levetiracetam and Lacosamide unchanged, maintain the adherence tools already working well, and retain buccal midazolam for prolonged convulsive seizures. We discussed SUDEP and the importance of supervision at night when unwell, ongoing adherence, and consistent sleep routine.

I have arranged routine blood monitoring today and will write with the results. I have booked a review in six months. Please do not hesitate to contact me sooner if there is any deterioration or concern.

With best wishes,

entities.text: only seven seizures so far this year
The family feel seizure frequency has decreased markedly since starting medication, with only seven seizures so far this year

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter states: "only seven seizures so far this year" as of the clinic date 20 May 2023. Interpreting "this year" from 1 Jan 2023 to 20 May 2023 is approximately 4.5 to 5 months. The most precise normalisable frequency is 7 seizures over this partial year window. Since the allowed output requires a per day/week/month/year unit, the closest fit is per year or per month. However, "so far this year" implies not a full year. Using months yields 7 over approximately 5 months. There is no other explicit numeric frequency that is higher. Therefore, the frequency is 7 per 5 months.
seizure_frequency_number: only seven seizures so far this year
reference: The family feel seizure frequency has decreased markedly since starting medication, with only seven seizures so far this year
